# Transcriﾃｧﾃ｣o: 05-The Most Common Deal Iﾃ｢ﾂﾂ况e Seen

- **Fonte:** 05-The Most Common Deal Iﾃ｢ﾂﾂ况e Seen

---

## [00:00 - 02:13]

For small business acquisitions, the most common deals can be structured with one-third cash down, one-third paid overtime, and then one-third in a balloon payment at the end of the payment term. Balloon payments are simply one large sum paid at the end of the note term. Frequently, funds for these deals are produced through a loan from the Small Business Administration or SBA. We'll be spending a lot of time on this in the following modules. This is the best for businesses that are already generating revenue with assets that can be leveraged in a strong operating history. And with bank loans or motivated sellers, they may finance the deal for 5-8% interest rates. Funds can also be requested through close personal friends and family, although I'll warn you this can make Thanksgiving or Christmas very uncomfortable if you do not perform. But, leveraging the assets through one of the LBO strategies we covered before would be a perfect scenario for this kind of business. If none of these are an option for you, then join a small business you want to buy as a senior sales role or operator role, like we discussed last lesson. Then structure an agreement with the owner in which you have the ability to massively drive revenue and credit the value you help create towards a potential down payment in the future for the business. Remember though, while this is the most common deal type I've seen, deals can come in all shapes and all sizes. It can be a 50-50, a 75-25. If you love a deal, don't get hung up on the structure, get hung up on the future value of the business you're buying. Over the next few lessons, we'll start to pivot to finding the ideal candidate, thinking about your exit before you structure a deal and enter to buy it, and whittling down the candidates who would be the best for you to buy the perfect business on this journey. I'll see you in the next lesson.
